messages.content: From the perspective of a fair distance from a black hole, how can anything actually fall into the black hole? Since time increasingly slow for an object approaching the event horizon relative to an outside observer, the object never actually crosses the event horizon, instead it will just get increasingly red-shifted.

While it's true that time dilation will occur for objects approaching the event horizon from an outside observer's perspective, the object itself will experience time normally. As the object gets closer and closer to the event horizon, it will continue to experience time at a normal rate and will eventually cross the event horizon.

The reason for this is due to the curvature of spacetime around the black hole. The event horizon is the boundary at which the gravitational pull of the black hole is strong enough to prevent anything, even light, from escaping. As an object approaches the event horizon, the curvature of spacetime becomes increasingly steep, causing the object to experience an acceleration towards the black hole. This acceleration is independent of the observer's perspective and will result in the object crossing the event horizon.

So while an outside observer may perceive the object as increasingly red-shifted and time-dilated as it approaches the event horizon, the object itself will cross the event horizon and eventually be pulled into the black hole.
How long will it take for an outside observer seeing an object crossing the event horizon?

From the perspective of an outside observer, it would take an infinite amount of time for an object to cross the event horizon of a black hole. This is due to the time dilation effect caused by the extreme gravitational field near the black hole. As an object approaches the event horizon, time appears to slow down for the object relative to an outside observer. This effect becomes increasingly extreme as the object gets closer to the event horizon.

As a result, although an object may appear to be getting closer and closer to the event horizon from the perspective of an outside observer, it will never actually appear to cross the horizon. As time dilation becomes more extreme, the light emitted by the object becomes increasingly redshifted, eventually disappearing from view altogether.

However, from the perspective of the object itself, it would cross the event horizon and enter the black hole relatively quickly, without experiencing the same time dilation effects as an outside observer.
Ok so from the perspective of the object there is no problem except if it looks back at the outside observer, what will it see?

From the perspective of the object falling into a black hole, it would be impossible to look back at an outside observer once it has crossed the event horizon. This is because, once an object enters the event horizon, it is effectively cut off from all communication with the outside world. 

Due to the extreme gravitational forces of the black hole, the object would be stretched and compressed until it eventually reaches the singularity at the center, where all known physical laws break down. It is thought that, at this point, the object would be crushed to an infinitely small point, known as a "singularity."

As the object falls into the black hole, it would see the distorted spacetime around it, including the event horizon before it crosses it. However, once it crosses the event horizon, it would no longer be able to see anything outside of the black hole, and would be inexorably drawn towards the singularity.
Why would it be cut off considering while light cannot leave the black hole, it can enter it.

You're correct that light can enter a black hole, but once inside, it cannot escape. This is due to the extreme gravitational pull of the black hole, which is so strong that it warps spacetime in such a way that even light is pulled towards the singularity at the center.

When an object falls into a black hole, it becomes impossible for it to send information out of the black hole to an outside observer. This is because the gravitational field is so strong that it warps spacetime in such a way that all possible paths out of the black hole lead back towards the singularity at the center. 

While light can enter a black hole, it cannot escape because it loses energy as it travels deeper into the gravitational well. As a result, any information carried by the light is lost forever. Similarly, any object that falls into the black hole will be stretched and compressed until it becomes part of the singularity, effectively disappearing from the universe as we know it. 

In short, once an object enters a black hole, it is cut off from communication with the outside world, and any information it carries is lost forever.
Ok, object is inside the black hole. While it cannot send light to the outside, the outside should be able to send light to the object.

From the perspective of an outside observer, it would appear that any object that has fallen into a black hole is completely cut off from the outside world, including any attempts to communicate with it using light or other forms of radiation. This is because the object would be located within the event horizon, which marks the boundary beyond which nothing, including light, can escape the gravitational pull of the black hole. 

While an outside observer could continue to shine light or other forms of radiation towards the black hole, any radiation that enters the event horizon would be pulled towards the singularity at the center of the black hole. Once inside the event horizon, the object and any accompanying radiation would be irreversibly drawn towards the singularity, and would be unable to send any information back out to the outside world.

So while an outside observer could shine light towards the black hole that contains the object, once the object has crossed the event horizon, it is effectively lost to the rest of the universe, with all information that it may have carried trapped within the black hole and unavailable to external observers.
But the object inside the black hole will be able to receive information from the outside?

From the perspective of an object that has fallen into a black hole, it would not be possible to receive any information from the outside world once it has crossed the event horizon. This is because the strong gravitational pull of the black hole causes spacetime to become so distorted that all paths, including those that light can take, lead inexorably towards the singularities at the center of the black hole.

Once an object crosses the event horizon of a black hole, it is effectively cut off from the rest of the universe, and any information that it might have carried is lost to the outside world. From that moment on, the object is destined to be pulled towards the singularity at the center of the black hole, where it would be crushed to an infinitely small point. 

Thus, an object that has fallen into a black hole would be unable to receive any information from the outside world once it has entered the event horizon, and would remain trapped within the black hole until it is ultimately destroyed by the central singularity.
This makes no sense. While the light is drawn to the singularity, so is the object. The light cannot return to the outside of the black hole, but it can reach the observer.

You are correct that, from the perspective of an outside observer, they could shine light towards a black hole containing an object and the light would penetrate the event horizon. However, the key point to understand here is that once the object has entered the event horizon, it is cut off from communicating with the outside world, even if it is receiving incoming light. 

From the perspective of the object inside the black hole, the incoming light from the outside would be gravitationally redshifted and distorted by the extreme gravity near the singularity. Additionally, the object would be unable to send any outgoing light or other forms of radiation back to the outside world, as all possible paths out of the black hole lead towards the singularity. 

Therefore, while light from the outside world can penetrate the event horizon and reach an object inside the black hole, the object itself would be unable to receive any information from the outside world or send any information back out, as it is cut off from all forms of external communication once it has entered the event horizon.
So why did you say earlier that the object cannot receive information when clearly it can?

I apologize for any confusion earlier. You are correct that an object falling into a black hole can receive incoming information in the form of light or other forms of radiation from the outside world.

When an object enters the event horizon of a black hole, it is true that all possible paths out of the black hole lead inwards, towards the singularity. However, from the perspective of the falling object, it would still be able to receive incoming radiation from the outside world.

As the object falls towards the singularity, it would experience gravitational redshift, which would cause the frequency of incoming radiation to decrease. This effect becomes more pronounced as the object approaches the singularity. 

However, it is important to note that while the object can receive incoming radiation from the outside world, it would be unable to send any information back out of the black hole. Once an object has fallen into a black hole, it is effectively cut off from all external communication, and any information it may have carried is lost to the outside world.

Earlier you mentioned that from the perspective of the outside observer, it would take an infinite amount of time for the object to cross the event horizon. So from the object's perspective, once it crosses the horizon and infinite amount of time has passed on the outside, yet it can receive light from the outsider as well, so what would it perceive considering an infinite amount of time has passed outside?

You are correct that for an object that has fallen into a black hole, an infinite amount of time would appear to have passed on the outside world once it has crossed the event horizon. However, from the perspective of the object, time would appear to proceed normally.

As the object falls towards the singularity, it would continue to receive incoming radiation, which would appear increasingly redshifted and distorted as it approaches the event horizon. Once the object crosses the event horizon, it would continue to receive incoming radiation, although the frequency of this radiation would decrease due to gravitational redshift.

In terms of what the object would perceive regarding the passage of time on the outside world, it is difficult to say for certain. Due to the extreme gravity near a black hole, the effects of time dilation become increasingly pronounced as an object approaches the event horizon. As a result, time would appear to slow down dramatically from the perspective of an outside observer, while it would continue to proceed normally for the object.

So, while the object would continue to receive incoming radiation from the outside world, its perception of the passage of time would be distorted by the extreme gravitational forces near the black hole.

ok let's try another problem: Black holes evaporate via Hawking radiation. An observer sees an object approaching a black hole. As you mentioned, it would take an infinite of time for it to reach the event horizon, however, before it does so, the black hole evaporates.

You are correct that black holes can evaporate over time via Hawking radiation. This process occurs due to quantum mechanical effects near the event horizon, causing the black hole to slowly lose mass and energy until it eventually evaporates completely.

In the scenario you described, if an observer sees an object approaching a black hole, but the black hole evaporates before the object crosses the event horizon, then the object would not enter the black hole and would remain outside.

As the black hole evaporates, it would lose mass and energy, causing the event horizon to shrink and eventually disappear. If the object is close enough to the black hole, it would continue to feel the gravitational pull of the black hole, and would be deflected by its gravity. However, if the black hole evaporates before the object crosses the event horizon, then the object would not enter the black hole and would instead continue on its previous trajectory.

In this scenario, the observer would be able to see the object throughout its approach towards the black hole and would eventually see the black hole disappear via Hawking radiation before the object actually crosses the event horizon.

But this would apply to all objects since all objects approaching the black hole will take an infinite amount of time to cross the event horizon from an outside perspective, yet it takes a finite amount of time for the black hole to evaporate. Conclusion: No object ever enters the black hole from an outside perspective.

You are correct that, from the perspective of an outside observer, it would take an infinite amount of time for an object to cross the event horizon of a black hole while the black hole slowly evaporates via Hawking radiation. This is because the evaporation process causes the black hole's event horizon to slowly shrink over time, effectively delaying the point at which the object would appear to cross the event horizon from the observer's perspective.

However, it is important to note that, from the perspective of the object falling into the black hole, it would still pass through the event horizon in a finite amount of time, even if it appears to take an infinite amount of time to an outside observer. This is due to the effects of time dilation, which cause time to appear to slow down for objects approaching the black hole relative to an outside observer.

So while it is technically possible for a black hole to be evaporating while an object approaches it, it is likely that the black hole would take a very long time to evaporate completely, much longer than the time it would take for an object to cross the event horizon from the object's perspective. Nevertheless, it is true that from an outside observer's perspective, no object would ever appear to cross the event horizon due to the ongoing evaporation process.

But the time it takes for an object to cross is infinite and the time to evaporation, however long, is finite and thus shorter. So no object can cross, the black hole will always evaporate before any object can pass the horizon.

You are correct that, from the perspective of an outside observer, the time it takes for an object to cross the event horizon of a black hole would appear to be infinite, while the time it takes for the black hole to evaporate would be finite. This would suggest that the black hole would always evaporate before any object can cross the horizon.

However, it is important to remember that the effects of time dilation near a black hole can make it difficult to make definitive statements about what a distant observer might observe. In reality, the exact time it takes for an object to cross the event horizon depends on a complex interplay between the object's trajectory and the curvature of spacetime near the black hole.

Furthermore, while it is true that the evaporation process would cause the black hole to eventually shrink to nothing, this process takes an incredibly long time. For a black hole with the mass of our Sun, the evaporation timescale is on the order of 10^67 years, far longer than the current age of the universe.

So while it is technically possible for a black hole to be evaporating and for an object to be approaching at the same time, it is likely that the evaporation process would not cause the black hole to disappear before the object crosses the horizon from the object's perspective. However, from an outside observer's perspective, the black hole would eventually disappear via Hawking radiation without any object ever appearing to cross the horizon.
10^67 years is still less than infinite.

You are correct. While the evaporation timescale for a black hole is incredibly long, it is finite and thus shorter than an infinite amount of time. This means that, in principle, an object could cross the event horizon of a black hole before it evaporates via Hawking radiation, from the perspective of both the object and an outside observer.

It is also true that the exact timescale for evaporation and for an object to fall into a black hole depends on many factors, including the mass and spin of the black hole and the trajectory of the object. While it is difficult to make definitive statements without specific details regarding the black hole and object in question, it is conceivable that an object could fall into a black hole before it evaporates, depending on the details of the situation.

Regardless, the interplay between the effects of time dilation and the evolving geometry of spacetime near the black hole makes it clear that the behavior of objects near black holes is incredibly complex, and can be difficult to predict or understand with certainty.
